title: Multiscale in vivo imaging of tumor evolution using a germline conditional triple-reporter mouse - Nature Biotechnology
text:
Download PDF

AbstractImaging reporter genes are essential tools in cancer research for monitoring tumor evolution, cell dissemination, gene activation and treatment response. However, germline reporter models typically rely on single imaging modalities operating over limited spatial scales. To address these limitations, we developed an inducible triple-reporter mouse model (Rosa26LSL-NRL) that integrates three reporters for complementary imaging modalities, fluorescence, bioluminescence and positron emission tomography (PET), along with inducible Cre-lox functionality for precise spatiotemporal control of reporter expression in genetically engineered mouse models. Using a multiscale, multimodal approach, we visualize deep tissue oncogenesis in models of hepatocellular carcinoma and lung adenocarcinoma and, guided by whole-body imaging with bioluminescence and [18F]tetrafluoroborate PET/magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), resolve cell−cell interactions within tumor microenvironments using in situ microscopy. This triple-reporter system enables multiscale investigation of biological processes within whole animals, facilitating sensitive, tissue-specific, in vivo cell tracking from whole-body to cellular resolution.

MainTo develop a comprehensive understanding of complex diseases such as cancer, methods are needed to correlate information between the cellular microscale and the whole-body macroscale in intact biological organisms. Whole-body imaging provides valuable information on tumor growth, metastasis and treatment response, but it lacks the resolution to image the cellular environment. Conversely, microscale imaging can reveal dynamic interactions between cancer cells and their microenvironment but without systemic context. A combined system would enable whole-body imaging to provide a contextual understanding of longitudinal tumor formation and then use microscopy for a deeper analysis of the cellular interactions during key events such as metastatic seeding or immune cell activation.Imaging reporter genes encode proteins that produce a sensitive and specific signal for in vivo imaging1. The signal can mark clonally expanding transformed cells and track them throughout a tumor’s progression and during treatment. This is particularly useful in the context of genetically engineered mouse models (GEMMs) of cancer2. These models frequently develop lesions at inaccessible locations and with unpredictable latency. The need to monitor tumor development in these mouse models has led to the development of strategies that combine tumor initiation with the induction of reporter transgene expression. For conditional GEMMs, this is often achieved with the Cre-loxP system3,4,5,6. Numerous reporter strains are widely used and are available for multiple modalities, working with bioluminescence7,8, fluorescence7,9 or radionuclide imaging10, but none of these covers the full range of spatial scales and tissue depths required for multiscale imaging.Conditional reporter systems could conceivably track tumor development from initiation; however, imaging modalities that visualize cellular events, such as intravital fluorescence microscopy, lack the penetration depth and field of view (FOV) to locate specific cell populations in many anatomical locations within an imaged subject11,12. Conversely, although whole-animal tomographic imaging modalities such as PET or MRI overcome penetration depth limitations, they lack the spatial resolution for cellular imaging13. These limitations suggest that it is unlikely that a single imaging modality could ever bridge the gap between macroscopic and microscopic imaging. Consequently, combination approaches can be transformative, by allowing a specific cell population to be marked and followed at the whole-animal scale over time while, crucially, retaining the capability to zoom in on microscopic processes within that same cell population at key timepoints in disease evolution.One way to achieve multiscale imaging is by engineering a fusion protein that incorporates reporter modules for both macro and micro imaging. Such a reporter could enable longitudinal monitoring of target cell populations using modalities such as PET or bioluminescence while also offering single-cell detection through intravital fluorescence microscopy14,15,16. Although imaging of triple-modality fusion reporters has previously been demonstrated in vivo, each reporter combination has required extensive optimization and characterization, indicating limited flexibility of this approach14,15. As these systems are constitutively active across all cells and tissues, they cannot track specific cell populations in vivo, a capability essential for visualizing disease initiation, monitoring progression and observing specific cellular interactions.Here, we developed and characterized a multiscale imaging system, R26LSL-NRL, that integrates three distinct reporter genes, encoding mouse sodium iodide symporter (mNIS)17 fused to tagged red fluorescent protein (TagRFP)18,19, which is co-expressed but separated from firefly luciferase (Luc2)20 by a self-cleaving porcine 2A (P2A) sequence. By combining these complementary reporters, the R26LSL-NRL mouse overcomes inherent limitations of single imaging modalities. We used the R26LSL-NRL mouse to demonstrate in vivo imaging of tumorigenesis across various spatial and temporal scales. This approach has the potential to unveil biologically important features, such as macroscopic growth kinetics and therapy response, while simultaneously capturing microscopic vascular and immune cell dynamics. The R26LSL-NRL reporter mouse establishes a platform for systemic, multimodal and multiscale imaging, enabling comprehensive visualization of biological processes across whole-body to cellular scales.Results
Rosa26 locus targeting and embryonic stem cell selection and characterizationTo generate an inducible germline triple-reporter system, we targeted the Rosa26 locus in HM1 mouse embryonic stem (mES) cells with a transgene element CAG-LSL-mNIS-RFP-P2A-Luc2, allowing Cre-dependent expression of a tripartite cDNA encoding a fusion of mNIS (Slc5a5) and RFP alongside Luc2 (Fig. 1). To demonstrate the function of mNIS and Luc2 in vitro, we performed radiolabeled pertechnetate ([99mTc]TcO4−) uptake and bioluminescence assays on the Cre-deleted NRL-ES-Cre lines (CRE 1−4) and parent NRL-ES cells. We observed an 8.2−11.4-fold induction of [99mTc]TcO4− uptake and a 137−364-fold induction of bioluminescence in the NRL-ES-Cre cell lines compared to the parent NRL-ES line (Extended Data Fig. 1a,b). These measurements confirmed that the transgene reporter construct integrated into the parent ES cell line’s genome and was switched on by Cre recombinase-mediated deletion of the lox-STOP-lox (LSL) sequence in the NRL-ES-Cre cells. We verified the location and copy number of the transgene using targeted locus amplification, sequencing and data mapping; these analyses showed that a single copy of the transgene cassette integrated correctly at the targeted location (mouse chr6:113,076,032−113,076,035) within an intron of Gt(ROSA)26Sor. Sections of tissue samples (n = 1 mouse for each group) were used for RNAscope detection, which was quantified using HALO analysis suite (positive stain H-score; Extended Data Fig. 3a,b). Abd., abdominal; p s−1 cm−2 sr−1, photons per second per square centimeter per steradian; TAM, tamoxifen.Whole-animal bioluminescence imaging (BLI) showed high light output across the whole body of NRL-CV (R26wt/LSL-NRL + CMV-cre) mice relative to the littermate NRL controls, indicating robust induction of Luc2 activity expression (Fig. 2b). After tamoxifen injection in NRL-CE animals and littermate NRL controls, we observed induction of Luc2 activity in all tissue types tested ranging from 31-fold (brain) to 8,756-fold (abdominal muscle) (Fig. 2c,d and Extended Data Fig. 2a).[18F]TFB PET/MRI showed widespread mNIS-mediated tracer uptake particularly evident in skeletal muscle and the heart of the NRL-CV animals in comparison to the NRL controls (Fig. 2e). Similarly, technetium pertechnetate ([99mTc]TcO4−) biodistribution indicated that average radioactivity uptake in skeletal muscle and heart of tamoxifen-treated NRL-CE animals was higher (ratio to control of 6.4-fold and 3.6-fold, respectively) when compared to NRL littermate controls (Extended Data Fig. 2b). For the remaining tissue types, average [99mTc]TcO4− uptakes appeared lower in NRL-CE animals (ratios to control in the range of 0.08−0.90). This was accompanied by radioactivity depletion from blood and urine (ratio to control of 0.06 and 0.10, respectively), consistent with rapid and avid uptake of the tracer by the large biomass of skeletal and cardiac muscle and the evidence of the ‘sink effect’23. The absolute level of [99mTc]TcO4− uptake in the brain was low for both experimental groups (Extended Data Fig. 2b), which can be attributed to the blood−brain barrier restricting the passage of the tracer from blood into the brain’s extracellular space24.RNAscope detection of mNIS (Slc5a5) mRNA revealed high levels of transcript induction across most tissue types (Fig. 2f). The positive stain H-score ratio of NRL-CV to NRL mice ranged from 2.8-fold in the thyroid to 383.7-fold in the pancreas. The NRL-CV spleen showed an H-score similar to its NRL littermate control (ratio of 1.33) (Fig. 2f and Extended Data Fig. 3a). Similarly, high levels of mNIS induction were observed across most tissue types after tamoxifen induction in NRL-CE mice although slightly less pronounced than in NRL-CV (Fig. 2f and Extended Data Fig. 3b). These values ranged from 2.5-fold induction in the brain to 22.4-fold in the heart. However, exceptions included the thyroid (ratio of 0.15) and lung (ratio of 1.02). Expression was uniform in the muscle (Fig. 2g), heart and pancreas but heterogeneous in the brain, liver and spleen (Extended Data Fig. 3c). To demonstrate induction in immune cells, we performed flow cytometry on blood from NRL-CV mice, showing TagRFP expression in 17.9 ± 4.0% of CD45+ cells (Fig. 2h and Extended Data Fig. 4a). Notably, among the leukocyte subsets assayed, most were negative for TagRFP expression, with the exception of neutrophils (49.4 ± 3.2%; Extended Data Fig. 4b). Taken together, these results show that a single copy of the R26LSL-NRL allele is inherited in a Mendelian fashion. LSL-MYC/LSL-NRL (NRL-BM) GEMM of hepatocellular carcinomaA critical advantage of developing an inducible multiscale imaging reporter system is its ability to track the presence, expansion and movement of cell populations over time. This is vitally important for imaging oncogenesis in autochthonous mouse models, where accurately determining the onset, growth and multiplicity of lesions is difficult, if not impossible. We, therefore, tested the utility of the R26LSL-NRL reporter allele in the context of tumor formation in a conditional mouse model of hepatocellular carcinoma combining two gain-of-function Cre-inducible alleles, Ctnnb1ex3/wt and Rosa26LSL-MYC/wt (BM)25,26. We induced liver tumors by intravenous injection of AAV8-TBG-Cre into NRL-BM and BM (littermate control) mice (Fig. 3a).Fig. 3: In vivo multimodal imaging of oncogenesis in the Ctnnb1ex3/wt/Rosa26LSL-MYC/LSL-NRL (NRL-BM) GEMM of hepatocellular carcinoma.Full size imagea, R26LSL-NRL reporter mice were crossed with two gain-of-function Cre-dependent alleles, Ctnnb1ex3/wt and Rosa26LSL-MYC/wt (BM), to generate a conditional mouse model of hepatocellular carcinoma. Liver tumors were induced by intravenous injection of AAV8-TBG-Cre into NRL-BM and BM (littermate control) mice with AAV8-TBG-Empty used as a control vector. b, Tumor growth time course was measured by BLI in a cohort of NRL-BM mice (n = 13) in which liver tumors were induced by an intravenous injection of AAV8-TBG-Cre vector shortly after the first imaging timepoint. Individual data points represent total photon flux (p s−1) measured for each animal at respective imaging timepoints from a constant-area ROI placed over the chest in Living Image 4.5 image analysis software. Mouse shown in c and d is labeled ‘c/d’ and mouse shown in f and g is labeled ‘f/g’. c, Example sequence of images acquired from an animal of the cohort described above prior to (0) and at 180 days, 210 days and 240 days from tumor induction. d, Example (same mouse as in c) static [18F]tetrafluoroborate ([18F]TFB) PET magnetic resonance image acquired from an NRL-BM animal 240 days after tumor induction. e, Quantification of [18F]TFB-PET in NRL-BM (n = 7 mice), BM (n = 4 mice) with AAV8-TBG-Cre-induced liver tumors and NRL-BM (n = 5 mice) receiving AAV8-TBG-Empty vector. Five mice underwent BLI but did not receive PET imaging due to lack of scanner availability. Each data point represents SUVmax for a liver tumor or a normal liver ROI, quantified using VivoQuant. Multiple ROIs were averaged to generate animal-level means; independent mice are color coded. Images were acquired 70−90 minutes after injection of [18F]TFB (approximately 500 MBq kg−1). Statistical comparisons were performed on animal-level mean values using log-normal ordinary one-way ANOVA. Homogeneity of variance was supported by the Brown−Forsythe test (P = 0.35). Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test showed significantly higher uptake in NRL-BM tumors compared to all other groups (P ≤ 0.01). AR, autoradiography; Bl, bladder; p s−1 cm−2 sr−1, photons per second per square centimeter per steradian; St, stomach; Th, thyroid; Tu, tumor.Using longitudinal BLI of Luc2 signal, we visualized and localized oncogenesis over 12 months after induction in NRL-BM mice (Fig. 3b,c). By contrast, both AAV8-TBG-Cre/BM and AAV8-TBG-Empty/NRL-BM control mice showed no increase in signal after 56 weeks (Extended Data Fig. 5a). Tumors developed with varying burden and latency. BLI facilitated the selection of animals for tomographic [18F]TFB PET/MRI imaging, which allowed for disease characterization with the precise location of R26NRL lesions co-registered with anatomical magnetic resonance images (Fig. 3d).The PET signal within the NRL-BM tumors was homogeneous, with PET quantification (maximum standard uptake value (SUVmax)) clearly high in the reporter gene-expressing tumors, unlike the background liver (Fig. 3e). The PET signal was also greater than in lesions developed by littermate BM controls and untransformed liver tissue of NRL-BM animals infected with AAV8-TBG-Empty control virus (Fig. 3e). [18F]TFB biodistribution (SUVmean) closely matched the PET imaging quantification (Extended Data Fig. 5b), consistent with homogeneous tracer uptake. The homogeneous distribution and high contrast against the surrounding liver was further confirmed by autoradiography analysis (Extended Data Fig. 6a). Specific expression and co-localization of NIS/Slc5a5 and Luc2 mRNA were confirmed using RNAscope detection in sections of tumors collected from NRL-BM and control BM mice (Extended Data Fig. 6b,c). There was no background NIS/Slc5a5 expression in the livers from NRL-BM or control BM and NRL-BM/Empty mice (Extended Data Fig. 5c).In the BM mouse model, tumors typically arise as multiple distinct liver lesions26. Incorporating tomographic radionuclide imaging alongside planar BLI in NRL-BM mice markedly enhanced tumor visualization. We directly compared tumor multiplicity and spatial distribution using BLI and [18F]TFB PET imaging in the same animals. Despite the co-expression of NIS and luciferase at the cellular level (Extended Data Fig. 6c) and similar whole-body signal intensity from both modalities (Extended Data Fig. 6d), BLI was unable to resolve or quantify individual liver lesions due to light scattering and absorption. BLI identified four regional hotspots with substantial overlap (Fig. 3f), whereas [18F]TFB PET imaging in the same mouse detected and quantified 20 spatially distinct liver lesions (Fig. 3g). Across seven mice, PET imaging resulted in a 281% increase in individual tumor delineation compared to BLI (Fig. 3h), with each lesion (ranging from 5.2 mm3 to 158 mm3) clearly defined and measurable (Fig. 3i). These findings demonstrate that combining the R26LSL-NRL allele with conditional oncogene activation enables effective tumor visualization using complementary imaging approaches, with [18F]TFB PET markedly improving localization, delineation and quantification.Triple-modality reporter gene imaging of the Rosa26

fl/fl (NRL-KP) GEMM of lung carcinomaHaving demonstrated the utility of the R26LSL-NRL reporter allele in a liver cancer context, we next evaluated its performance in a conditional mouse model of non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma. This was achieved by combining R26LSL-NRL with a heterozygous conditional gain-of-function KrasLSL-G12D allele and a homozygous loss-of-function Trp53fl/fl allele (KP) (Fig. 4a). In contrast to the BM liver cancer model, where relatively large lesions in accessible locations can be monitored by palpation, tumor tracking in autochthonous lung cancer models like the KP model is more challenging. These models typically rely on the emergence of clinical symptoms, which often occur only at advanced stages. Anatomical imaging is further complicated by respiratory motion, proximity to the heart and the complexity of pulmonary vasculature and airways. AV, adenoviral vector; Bl, bladder; p s−1 cm−2 sr−1, photons per second per square centimeter per steradian; St, stomach; Tu, tumor.All KP animals administered Ad5CMVCre virus by intranasal inhalation developed lung lesions, which were longitudinally monitored by BLI for up to 120 days after induction (Fig. 4b,c). [18F]TFB PET imaging enabled high-contrast visualization of individual lung lesions relative to background signal (Fig. 4d). Quantitative analysis revealed that the average tumor SUVmax was significantly higher in NRL-KP mice compared to the KP littermate controls (15.01 versus 6.54 SUVmax, two-tailed nested t-test, P = 0.024) (Fig. 4e). RNAscope analysis of Slc5a5 showed significantly elevated H-scores in NRL-KP mice relative to KP controls (log-transformed nested t-test, P = 0.043), equivalent to an approximately 2.8-fold difference (95% confidence interval: 1.05–7.53×) (Fig. 4f,g). Luc2 RNAscope was additionally performed to qualitatively confirm transgene expression, supporting the presence of the reporter signal in NRL-KP tumors. Expression of Slc5a5 and Luc2 was heterogeneous but correlated both within and between tumors, indicating variable yet concordant levels of mNIS and luciferase expression (Extended Data Fig. 6c). Notably, we observed spontaneous NIS expression in some KP tumors and occasional loss of reporter expression in NRL-KP lesions. Despite this heterogeneity, the combination of high-specificity BLI and high-contrast tomographic [18F]TFB PET/MRI enabled accurate detection and anatomical localization of NRL-KP lesions, including nodules as small as 1.2 mm in diameter (approximately 904 μg wet tissue weight) (Extended Data Fig. 7).Tumor multiplicity, measured on histological sections, did not differ significantly between NRL-KP and KP cohorts (mean 22, range 14–35 versus mean 26, range 12–35 tumors per lung section; Mann–Whitney U-test, P = 0.77), although the small sample size (n = 4) means that modest differences between groups cannot be excluded.To determine if tumor cells detected by BLI and [18F]TFB-PET imaging could also be visualized by fluorescence microscopy, we induced tumors in two NRL-KP mice 12–16 weeks before imaging with BLI and [18F]TFB PET/MRI. After imaging, fixed precision-cut lung slices (PCLSs) were produced for fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 5a,b). Analysis of 14 spatially distinct tumor lesions across the two mice revealed that 57.8 ± 21.9% of the tumor areas exhibited high TagRFP fluorescence (Fig. 5c). On average, the TagRFP fluorescence signal in tumor tissue was 1.95 ± 0.36 times higher than in adjacent nontumor lung tissue (Fig. 5d). Co-staining with CD45-AF647 showed no apparent correlation between the abundance of TagRFP-high cells and leukocyte infiltration (simple linear regression r2 = 0.035, P = 0.52; Spearmanʼs r = 0.077, P = 0.80; Fig. 5e). Additionally, nonlinear analyses, including one-phase exponential and sigmoidal 4-parameter logistic (4PL) models, also showed poor fits (R2 ≤ 0.38) with unstable parameter estimates, suggesting the absence of threshold-like or cooperative leukocyte responses. These findings indicate that leukocytes do not preferentially infiltrate highly TagRFP-labeled tumors and are consistent with the reporter gene not eliciting a detectable immune response in the R26LSL-NRL model.Fig. 5: Fluorescence microscopy in the NRL-KP (KrasLSL-G12D/wt/Trp53fl/fl) GEMM of lung cancer.Full size imagea, R26LSL-NRL reporter mice were crossed with the gain-of-function KrasLSL-G12D and the loss-of-function Trp53fl/fl Cre-dependent alleles (KP) to generate a conditional mouse model of lung adenocarcinoma. Tumor cells are visible due to TagRFP (yellow). Scale bar, 100 µm. c,d, Percentage of high RFP signal area (c) and tumor-to-normal RFP mean intensity ratios (d) (n = 14 tumors from two mice). Data are shown as box plots with overlaid individual points; center line, median; box, interquartile range; whiskers, minimum to maximum. MI, mean intensity. e, High TagRFP expression versus leukocyte infiltration, both quantified as a percentage of tumor area (n = 14 tumors from two mice; independent mice are shown in different colors). Linear regression is shown (r2 = 0.035, P = 0.52; two-tailed Spearmanʼs r = 0.077, 95% confidence interval: −0.49 to 0.60, exact P = 0.80); nonlinear fits were also tested but are not displayed owing to poor fit and unstable parameter estimates. f, Live PCLSs. Single frames from videos (Supplementary Videos 1 and 2) of PCLSs harvested from KP or NRL-KP littermates, showing Hoechst 33342 (blue), anti-CD31 (green), anti-CD45 (red) and TagRFP (yellow). Scale bar, 50 µm.To visualize immune cell dynamics, we stained live PCLSs from one KP tumor and one NRL-KP tumor with fluorescently tagged antibodies targeting endothelial cells (anti-CD31 AF647) and leukocytes (anti-CD45 AF488) (Fig. 5f). These experiments demonstrated the feasibility of imaging cell−cell interactions between endothelial and immune cells within the preserved tumor microenvironment, where tumor cells were clearly identified by their TagRFP fluorescence (Fig. 5f and Supplementary Videos 1 and 2). This approach is particularly helpful for tumor models lacking specific surface antigens suitable for antibody tagging, as the fluorescent reporter enables reliable segmentation of tumor cells for image analysis. Although these observations were made ex vivo, the use of live PCLSs preserves native architecture and cellular context, allowing dynamic visualization of local interactions. Collectively, these data show that the R26LSL-NRL reporter allele enables tracking of marked cells during oncogenesis at the whole-animal level using BLI, localization and quantification of specific tumors using PET imaging and high-resolution fluorescent microscopy of cancer cells within these tumors and their spatial relationships with components of the local tumor microenvironment.Multiscale reporter gene imaging of the Rosa26

LSL-MYC/LSL-NRL (NRL-BM) GEMMs of lung and hepatocellular carcinomaTo evaluate whether BLI and PET could effectively stratify mice for intravital microscopy (IVM) by precisely identifying and localizing reporter signal-positive lung lesions, we induced a small cohort of NRL-KP (n = 4) animals with a low-titer dose of Ad5CMVCre (5 × 106 plaque-forming units (PFU)). This generated multiple tumors dispersed throughout the lung while limiting overall tumor multiplicity. In one NRL-KP animal, serial BLI and [18F]TFB PET/MRI revealed a lesion adjacent to the rib cage. To optimize access, marker pen dots (approximately 3 mm in diameter) were applied to the skin during a BLI session. These studies successfully demonstrated the feasibility of using the triple-reporter system to guide tumor-cell imaging within the tumor microenvironment, spanning whole-animal to cellular resolution, and underscored the usefulness of stratifying animals based on the anatomical accessibility of tumor sites.DiscussionThis study addresses a critical gap in the arsenal of tools for reporter gene imaging, which is the need within a single reporter system to flexibly switch imaging scales from whole-animal to cellular or even subcellular resolution, thereby fully exploiting the advantages of multimodal imaging. We optimize the application of genetically engineered mouse models, which faithfully recapitulate human pathogenesis but present challenges for visualizing systemic cellular dissemination. To achieve this, we developed a germline transgenic reporter cassette enabling multimodal imaging. This cassette encodes a self-cleaving mNIS-TagRFP-P2A-Luc2 sequence, driven by the CMV early enhancer/chicken β-actin (CAG) promoter, incorporating a Cre-dependent conditional stop element (LSL). We demonstrated the Cre-dependent inducibility of functional multimodal imaging by crossing the R26LSL-NRL mice with lines ubiquitously expressing Cre recombinase, such as the CMV-cre line with constitutively active Cre21 or the R26cre-ERT2 line expressing tamoxifen-inducible CreERT2 recombinase22. Consistently, bioluminescence and [18F]TFB PET signals were observed in every organ, confirming high-level, stable R26NRL expression, which enabled effective multimodality imaging across diverse tissue types.To our knowledge, multiscale or tomographic imaging of oncogenesis has not been previously demonstrated. We employed the R26LSL-NRL multimodal reporter system to visualize and monitor tumor development across multiple spontaneously arising tumors from distinct tissue origins. These lesions are typically difficult to detect with palpation or other imaging modalities due to their heterogeneous latency and deep tissue location6. Although BLI facilitated rapid screening, PET imaging provided specific tomographic tumor detection and quantification, enabling precise localization of individual tumors in three-dimensional space. Integration of whole-body mapping with IVM allowed spatial triangulation of tumor sites, enabling high-resolution in situ imaging of dynamic tumor−microenvironment interactions. This multiscale approach facilitated the identification, characterization and longitudinal monitoring of specific tumors as well as visualization of tumor–immune cell interactions. Immune cell abundance did not correlate with TagRFP expression, suggesting that the immune response was not enhanced in reporter-tagged tumors. As reporter transgene expression had been incorporated through the germline, we predicted that it would not be immunogenic.This conditional triple-reporter system offers several advantages over alternative approaches. For example, although somatic transfer of both Cre and reporter gene sequences into a conditional GEMM is resource-efficient, the approach is limited by vector tropism27 and payload constraints, with lentiviral vectors having a packaging limit of approximately 10 kilobases28. This restricts the number of reporter genes that can be delivered somatically. Although combining different independent transgenic alleles in a single composite animal through cross-breeding is possible, it is multistep, which results in prolonged and costly breeding. The R26LSL-NRL mouse has all reporter components at a single heritable locus. Moreover, the Rosa26 ‘safe harbor’ genomic locus has been used frequently by others to generate transgenic mice with predictable transgenic performance. This may limit the ability to combine R26LSL-NRL with a subset of cancer models8,9,10,29. Although alternative loci and transgenic technologies, such as CRISPR−Cas9, may alleviate this, cloning into Rosa26 was attractive as establishing and characterizing new reporter strains is both time-consuming and costly.We demonstrated that multimodal imaging of labeled cells with our triple-reporter mouse facilitates sensitive tumor cell imaging at different scales. Rather than fusing all constituent reporter transgenes together and risking compromised performance, we chose to employ a 2A sequence-mediated ribosome skipping technology to ensure near equimolar expression of all reporter transgenes. NIS function is not negatively impacted by fusing monomeric TagRFP or monomeric GFP to its C terminus19. We employed that strategy here, as, in addition to conferring a fluorescent readout to labeled cells, it enables visualization of the intracellular location of NIS protein, which is functional only when present in the cellular plasma membrane. Switching of the Cre-dependent reporter cassette marked the majority of the lesions with reporter transgene expression; however, a subset of tumors did not produce a detectable increase in signal. This may be an effect of incomplete recombination at induction, wherein successful Cre recombination occurs at the proto-oncogenic Cre-loxP loci but not at the R26LSL-NRL locus in this specific model.The switchable expression and targeted imaging of various tissues in the R26LSL-NRL mouse enable multiscale cell tracking in diverse areas, including developmental biology, neuroscience, immunology, oncology and regenerative medicine. Labeling immune cells opens up various possibilities—for example, crossing the R26LSL-NRL mouse with a line expressing Cre recombinase specifically in immune cells, such as an Ly6g-cre line (lymphocyte antigen 6 complex locus G), could enable imaging of the role of neutrophils in the pathogenesis of inflammatory arthritis and cancer30,31. In oncology, this reporter system could be used to track a wide variety of cell types and tumor cells during evolution from oncogenesis to metastatic seeding as well as for monitoring therapeutic interventions and evaluating treatment efficacy at whole-body and cellular scales in real time32. The R26LSL-NRL model allows for utilization of both optical and radionuclide approaches, overcoming the limitations associated with each of these as single modalities. This versatility allows complementary improvements in quantitative accuracy, tomography and high-throughput imaging as well as detailed visualization of cellular interactions. For example, when coupled with the oncogenic Kras- and Trp53-null lung cancer model, which, despite its inherent intertumor heterogeneity, is widely used for modeling non-small cell lung cancer33,34, the R26LSL-NRL system enables the imaging and monitoring of discrete tumors. We recently demonstrated rapid assessment of tumor response to therapy using mNIS; therefore, this reporter mouse strain offers the opportunity to characterize individual tumor treatment responses and heterogeneous tumor vulnerabilities in lung cancer32.
